text: highly vaunted achievements of civilization, universal education and modern publicity, instead of raising the level of culture, appear ultimately to produce certain symptoms of cultural devitalisation and degeneration (ibid.: 78). There are not a few parallels with the life and work of Norbert Elias, and they go far beyond the mere fact that Elias finished The Civilizing Process just a few months after Huizinga’s masterpiece. Both Huizinga and Elias realized the urgent need to understand, historically, what was happening with ‘culture’. Chapter IV in In The Shadow of Tomorrow is an attempt to identify Fundamentals of Culture (its title). However, rather than going straight to the substance, Huizinga – just like Elias – enters a discussion of the etymology of the word ‘culture’ and how it has developed within the variety of European languages, with a focus on German. 4 In 1941 Huizinga gave a speech in which he criticized German influences on Dutch science and was arrested by the Nazis. He was released in 1942 but not allowed to return to Leiden. Huizinga died in detention at De Steeg in Gerderland, near Arnheim, on 1 February 1945. Liminality and the Modern 148 Also, Huizinga takes notice of the common root in Greek of the words for culture (paideia) and play (paidia); a discussion which greatly inspired Voegelin, and which he incorporated in his own, very central, discussion of Plato’s The Laws (1974). In The Civilizing Process Elias took inspiration from Huizinga’s work, especially Erasmus and The Waning of the Middle Ages. Huizinga’s rendering of ordinary social relations and the psychic, inter-personal aspects of life in the Middle Ages are clearly present in Elias’ analysis, and surely more so than the brevity of Elias’ direct references to Huizinga may indicate. As van Krieken argues (1998: 23), Elias’ work is in fact best understood when read alongside Huizinga. It is also no coincidence that the element of play in culture and leisure was one of the explicit research topics later taken up by Elias, together with Eric Dunning. Elias surely understands the pivotal role of play and ritual; but does he actually theorize it well? Elias never says it this way, but if one considers what actually goes in within the European court cultures, pace his own description, the entire development can best be described as a ceremonial stifling of the element of play (Burke 2009), an excessive culture of play in which even the absolute King comes to stand powerless, caught in his own ceremonial game. 5 Caillos himself indicates something similar in his table summing up the corruption of games: carnival and theatre are marginal cultural forms of mimicry, while uniforms and ceremonial etiquette exemplify institutional forms of mimicry and simulation integrated into social life (Caillois 2001: 54). Alienation and split personality are the ultimate corrupted forms of mimicry at the levels of self and life-conduct. In his discussion of Elias, Mennell sums up how the aristocracy in court society became ‘specialists in the arts of consumption entrapped in a system of fine distinctions, status battles, and competitive expenditure from which they could not escape because their whole social identity depended upon it’ (Mennell as quoted in Featherstone 1992: 277). What developed was a still more tight and narrow frame for behaviour, dress and manners (Elias 1994: Part Two). Far from a genuine ‘civilizing process’ of deepening subjectivity and differentiation of consciousness (as Voegelin would have it), far from an expansion of human horizons within an experience of the transcendent (as Huizinga would have it), the court culture that Elias identifies as pivotal for modern rationality should rather be seen as a ritual passage stuck in the liminal phase: an endless game of strategic calculation, a continuous testing, a permanent use of exaggerated paraphernalia, funny hats and socks that are never taken off: an entrapment in a game that never ceases. It is arguably this stifled spirit and fossilized play-element that via the European court culture somehow came to permeate modern bourgeois rationality. A central part of the gaming culture that emerges in court culture has to do with role-playing; and it is Elias’ argument that it is this kind of role-playing that spreads beyond the walls of court culture. 5 It must be noted that in this structured positioning, the absolute King in many ways resembles today’s celebrities. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 149 Huizinga, Voegelin and Bateson: Framing Play In his analysis of play, Huizinga starts from anthropological foundations. In other words, he starts from Plato. In his assessment of Huizinga, Voegelin (1948) appreciates how Huizinga’s work is actually not ‘new’; what Huizinga does it to retrieve a theme from classical philosophy which had been lost, or which had become muddled. 6 Play is crucial for social, cognitive and emotional development. It matters for how we feel, for how we think, and for how we live together as human beings in a meaningful way as part of a social environment. Without play there can be no culture, no knowledge, no sentiment. Play is literally fundamental ‘in the sense that it cannot be reduced to another factor. Neither must play be interpreted in a utilitarian manner as serving a purpose, nor must its meaning be derived from the content that it presents; either attempt would destroy the independent meaning of play’ (Voegelin 1974: 311). For Voegelin as for Huizinga, it is the non-utilitarian character of play, music and rhythm that allows it to become ‘a source for the creation of new worlds of meaning beyond the everyday world’ (ibid.). Voegelin follows up on Huizinga’s presentation, stressing the way that play is an ‘overflow’ beyond the ordinary, normal existence: it is a central source of transcendence, and thus a vehicle of cultural growth; or, to use a different terminology, play has liminal features, existing in the world of the in-between, opening up new horizons – if treated carefully. And that is why we have to take it seriously. Play existed before Homo Sapiens –
